summary: Joel Spolsky describes the design and construction of Fog Creek Software's new office at 535 8th Avenue in New York, built to his exacting specifications for a software development environment. Working with architect Roy Leone, he prioritized private offices, abundant power outlets, flexible wiring, pair programming support, eye-rest sightlines, and a hangout atmosphere. The office features translucent acrylic walls for privacy with natural light, twenty outlets per desk with UPS-backed power, Snake Tray cable management, straight desks for collaboration, and clever interior windows that give every office three-sided light. Spolsky argues that investing in premium office space at $700/month per employee is justified because it attracts top-tier programming talent, which is essential for competing against lower-cost labor markets.
key_points: Private offices with doors are non-negotiable for programmer productivity, and the design uses translucent acrylic walls to balance privacy with natural light
Infrastructure details matter enormously: twenty power outlets per desk, accessible cable trays, and eight-port network switches per office eliminate daily friction
Office design should facilitate pair programming through straight desks rather than L-shaped corners that force awkward collaboration positions
Clever architectural tricks like angled interior windows give every developer a corner-office experience with light on two sides, without sacrificing privacy
Premium office space at $700/employee/month is a recruiting investment that helps attract 99.9th percentile developers needed to compete against lower-cost markets
money_quotes: Office space seems to be the one thing that nobody can get right and nobody can do anything about.
If I have to compete at New York salaries against Bangalore salaries, I'm going to need those superstars, so when people come in for an interview, I need to see jaws on the floor. It's about drama.

Your business success will depend on the extent to which programmers essentially live at your office. For this to be a common choice, your office had better be nicer than the average programmer's home.
I sneer at silly building managers who still think that one LAN port per office is about right. For lawyers, maybe.

Not only did we get spacious, windowed private offices, but even the common area workstations are hidden in clever angular alcoves, so everyone gets their own private space without line of sight to anyone else.
This is quite an accomplishment: you try coming up with a scheme to give everyone a corner office in a conventional building.
key_insight: Investing in thoughtfully designed private offices with superior infrastructure is not an extravagance but a competitive recruiting advantage that pays for itself by attracting elite programming talent.
